inline_review:
commit 72d03ab8b5efa9ee8a49e09d1040ad596cba8991
Author: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>
Subject: mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE

This commit disables DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE actions when
the kernel is compiled without the TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE option.

> diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h
> index 3a441fbca170..b3fcbe84b5d7 100644
> --- a/include/linux/damon.h
> +++ b/include/linux/damon.h
[ ... ]
> @@ -138,8 +138,10 @@ enum damos_action {
>  	DAMOS_WILLNEED,
>  	DAMOS_COLD,
>  	DAMOS_PAGEOUT,
> +#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
>  	DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
>  	DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
> +#endif
>  	DAMOS_LRU_PRIO,
>  	DAMOS_LRU_DEPRIO,
>  	DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT,
>  	DAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD,
>  	DAMOS_STAT,

Does adding this #ifdef shift the integer values of subsequent actions like
DAMOS_STAT when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is disabled?

The kernel selftest tools/testing/selftests/damon/sysfs.py uses drgn to read
the internal damos->action enum value and expects hardcoded integer values
(such as 'stat': 9). 

Additionally, modifying enum layouts based on kernel configuration can break
BPF and drgn tracing tools that rely on stable DWARF/BTF types.

Target Commit:
commit 72d03ab8b5efa9ee8a49e09d1040ad596cba8991
Author: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>
Date:   Sat Mar 14 09:38:12 2026 +0000

    mm/damon: guard THP-specific DAMOS actions with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
    
    MADV_HUGEPAGE and MADV_NOHUGEPAGE are guarded and they are not
    available when compiling the kernel without TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
    option. Therefore, DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE shouldn't be
    available to the user either.
    
    Signed-off-by: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

diff --git a/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst b/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst
index 29fff20b3c2a..207219ee10d4 100644
--- a/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst
+++ b/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst
@@ -460,9 +460,11 @@ that supports each action are as below.
  - ``pageout``: Reclaim the region.
    Supported by ``vaddr``, ``fvaddr`` and ``paddr`` operations set.
  - ``hugepage``: Call ``madvise()`` for the region with ``MADV_HUGEPAGE``.
-   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set.
+   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set when
+   TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is enabled.
  - ``nohugepage``: Call ``madvise()`` for the region with ``MADV_NOHUGEPAGE``.
-   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set.
+   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set when
+   TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is enabled.
  - ``lru_prio``: Prioritize the region on its LRU lists.
    Supported by ``paddr`` operations set.
  - ``lru_deprio``: Deprioritize the region on its LRU lists.
diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h
index 3a441fbca170..b3fcbe84b5d7 100644
--- a/include/linux/damon.h
+++ b/include/linux/damon.h
@@ -138,8 +138,10 @@ enum damos_action {
 	DAMOS_WILLNEED,
 	DAMOS_COLD,
 	DAMOS_PAGEOUT,
+#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
 	DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
 	DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
+#endif
 	DAMOS_LRU_PRIO,
 	DAMOS_LRU_DEPRIO,
 	DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT,
diff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c b/mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c
index 5186966dafb3..05e57c7ca2b6 100644
--- a/mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c
+++ b/mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c
@@ -2033,6 +2033,7 @@ static struct damos_sysfs_action_name damos_sysfs_action_names[] = {
 		.action = DAMOS_PAGEOUT,
 		.name = "pageout",
 	},
+#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
 	{
 		.action = DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
 		.name = "hugepage",
@@ -2041,6 +2042,7 @@ static struct damos_sysfs_action_name damos_sysfs_action_names[] = {
 		.action = DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
 		.name = "nohugepage",
 	},
+#endif
 	{
 		.action = DAMOS_LRU_PRIO,
 		.name = "lru_prio",
diff --git a/mm/damon/vaddr.c b/mm/damon/vaddr.c
index b069dbc7e3d2..19c488660027 100644
--- a/mm/damon/vaddr.c
+++ b/mm/damon/vaddr.c
@@ -897,12 +897,14 @@ static unsigned long damon_va_apply_scheme(struct damon_ctx *ctx,
 	case DAMOS_PAGEOUT:
 		madv_action = MADV_PAGEOUT;
 		break;
+#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
 	case DAMOS_HUGEPAGE:
 		madv_action = MADV_HUGEPAGE;
 		break;
 	case DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE:
 		madv_action = MADV_NOHUGEPAGE;
 		break;
+#endif
 	case DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT:
 	case DAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD:
 		return damos_va_migrate(t, r, scheme, sz_filter_passed);

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analyzing the Potential UAPI Change in DAMOS Hugepage Handling**

Okay, so I'm looking at this commit, and it looks like nobody's actually *using* `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` directly in any tests, which is a good starting point. But this commit is putting an `#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` around the code related to `hugepage` actions. My immediate concern is, is this a breaking change for userspace?

The core of it is the `action` sysfs interface. If a userspace tool is expecting to configure hugepage behavior through sysfs, even when transparent hugepages are disabled (which, by the way, should be fairly uncommon), that write to the `action` file is going to fail with `-EINVAL`. Before, the write would have succeeded, but the action wouldn't have actually *done* anything. `MADV_HUGEPAGE` would have been passed to `do_madvise`, which would fail because `madvise_behavior_valid` would return false in this case. But... wait, thinking through the control flow here... if `do_madvise` fails, it returns an error code, and `damos_madvise` checks if it's non-zero.
```c
	applied = do_madvise(mm, start, len, behavior) ? 0 : len;
```
If it's zero, `applied` also ends up as `0`. So, effectively, the action would just be marked as failed – zero bytes applied. This would be tracked silently in `damos_stat`. That's an important detail. So, in the past, userspace *could* configure "hugepage", but it would always fail to apply. Now, the sysfs is rejecting that upfront.

From a UAPI perspective, this is probably *better*. Rejecting unsupported features with `-EINVAL` is more explicit and less ambiguous than silently failing. It's technically a change, but returning `-EINVAL` for an unsupported feature is standard behavior.

However, I need to consider all the angles. What if a userspace tool exists that blindly iterates through all DAMOS actions, perhaps reading them from some sysfs or trace point? But, isn't `action` write-and-read? There's no sysfs file that lists *available* actions, is there? Wait... is there an `avail_actions` sysfs file or something similar that I'm forgetting about? That's what I need to investigate now.

    "thought": "**DAMON Action Enum and Sysfs Behavior Analysis**

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at the `action_show` and `action_store` functions in the DAMON sysfs interface. The code appears straightforward: `action_show` reads the current action from a scheme and converts it to a string for sysfs, while `action_store` does the reverse – taking a string from sysfs and setting the scheme's action. The key is how this interacts with `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`.

If `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled, `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` are effectively removed from the `enum damos_action` and therefore from `damos_sysfs_action_names`. Reading an invalid action returns -EINVAL, which is as expected. However, reading the action attribute in sysfs for a newly created scheme is crucial: it defaults to `DAMOS_STAT`, which is still valid regardless of the configuration. So, all is well there.

Now, with `action_store`, writing "hugepage" when `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is off *should* result in -EINVAL, which is exactly the behavior implemented. This is a good thing – it prevents users from configuring actions that aren't supported.

This commit, then, slightly tightens the ABI by *rejecting* unsupported actions, where previously they were silently ignored. That's a reasonable bug fix, preventing misconfigurations.

Next, I need to ensure that the `#ifdef` around `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` doesn't cause any hidden problems by changing the numeric values of subsequent `enum` members. I've got to check where the `enum damos_action` values are actually used.

From my analysis, it looks like the values are primarily used:

1.  Internally in `sysfs-schemes.c` for name-to-value mapping.
2.  In `switch` statements within DAMON core (`vaddr.c`, `paddr.c`, `core.c`, etc.).
3.  In tests (`core-kunit.h`).
4.  By in-kernel modules (`reclaim.c`, `lru_sort.c`).

Crucially, no external user ABI exposes these numeric values. Tracepoints log `scheme_idx` or `target_id`, not the `action`. Therefore, conditionally excluding members from the `enum` is safe because the numeric values are not exposed, preventing an issue if a kernel module is compiled with a different `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` than the kernel.

Now, a quick scan of the code finds that `NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS` isn't used to size any externally exposed array, so that's safe. A quick scan of `mm/damon/core.c` for `NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS` or `action` confirms this.

Finally, let's verify there are no gotchas in `include/linux/damon.h`, and that trace events don't rely on fixed enum values. I've checked for `NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS` use, and it's clean. The trace event definitions do not contain the action field, and any parsing scripts don't expect specific enum values. So, it all looks good.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` ABI Impact**

Alright, my initial assessment is that conditionally enabling `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` shouldn't introduce any ABI breakage. The `enum` value itself, and the string "hugepage", are shielded by the same preprocessor directive (`#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`) in `damos_sysfs_action_names[]`. This means the string representation is controlled alongside the enum definition.

Now, let me double-check `include/linux/damon.h`. I need to be absolutely sure. Okay, here it is:

```c
enum damos_action {
	DAMOS_WILLNEED,
	DAMOS_COLD,
	DAMOS_PAGEOUT,
#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
	DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
	DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
#endif
	DAMOS_LRU_PRIO,
	DAMOS_LRU_DEPRIO,
	DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT,
	DAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD,
	DAMOS_STAT,		/* Do nothing but only record the stat */
	NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS,
};
```

The crucial question is, does anything *rely* on the numerical value of `NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS` or its size? Tracepoints are out; they’re string-based. Sysfs is also string-based.

However, what if some *user* accidentally passes an integer via a debugfs interface? DAMON debugfs is deprecated (a good thing!) but let's be thorough. The code might still be lurking somewhere. I need to check `mm/damon/dbgfs.c` (or its equivalent if it was removed), to see if it even exists anymore. That's the real potential weakness here.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Damon sysfs Interface and MADV_HUGEPAGE Behavior**

Okay, so I'm diving into this `damos` sysfs interface. My initial thought is to understand how it handles the `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` actions, especially in relation to the `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` kernel configuration. The interface uses this `damos_sysfs_action_names` array, which maps user-provided strings (like "hugepage") to internal `enum` values (like `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE`). The `action_store` function is what translates the user input strings, and `action_show` converts the internal enum value back to a string for the user. Seems straightforward, with the check in place that prevents a user from selecting "hugepage" if THP isn't enabled. That makes sense. If someone tries to set "hugepage" when the config isn't set, it returns `-EINVAL`, as expected. And the read path iterates to return the corresponding name.

But then, the thought hits that `damos_action` is an enum, so that's good. But what if a kernel module, like `mm/damon/reclaim.c`, doesn't *define* `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` because of a conditional compile? It *shouldn't* be an issue because I already know that kernel components only use `DAMOS_PAGEOUT`, `DAMOS_LRU_PRIO`, etc. However, I want to rule out a subtle, weird issue in the code, because there is no exported `NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS` to userspace. I've already searched for that and array sizes indexed by `DAMOS_*` and found nothing. I have to be thorough.

Then, my mind jumps to `MADV_HUGEPAGE` and `MADV_NOHUGEPAGE`, the madvise hints. Are *those* guarded by the same configuration? The patch claims they are, but something feels off. My experience with the kernel tells me to *always* double-check claims like that, especially when it comes to uAPI headers. Ah, the "pre_fetched_context" confirms that they are defined as constants *always*:
```c
#define MADV_HUGEPAGE	14		/* Worth backing with hugepages */
#define MADV_NOHUGEPAGE	15		/* Not worth backing with hugepages */
```
The patch *and* the commit message claims that they are guarded, but that's just not true. If I read `mman-common.h` in `<uapi/asm-generic/` there is NO `#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` around those defines! So, the user-space headers always define these `MADV_` constants. But the question is: what is the *kernel* behavior? The claim just doesn't seem right, even though the messages say that. I need to dive into the `madvise()` system call implementation or its usage to understand the kernel's behavior *when* THP is disabled. I'm going to check `mm/madvise.c` for `MADV_HUGEPAGE` to see what actually happens in the kernel code. This requires more investigation.

    "thought": "**DAMON sysfs and CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE: A Deep Dive**

Okay, so I'm looking at this DAMON code, specifically the sysfs interaction with hugepages. The initial issue seemed to be that writing "hugepage" to the sysfs node was returning `-EINVAL`. Digging into `mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c`, I initially thought the sysfs code itself was isolated, so the problem *shouldn't* be there. The `MADV_HUGEPAGE` enum is guarded by `#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`, which *feels* like a likely source of trouble.

Then, a thought: Could line 2038 of `mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c` be the issue? The `action` array *does* change size based on the config. I looked at the code...

```c
#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
	{
		.action = DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
		.name = "hugepage",
	},
	{
		.action = DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
		.name = "nohugepage",
	},
#endif
```

If we disable `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`, the array *does* get shorter. However, the `enum damos_action` in `include/linux/damon.h` also shrinks.

```c
#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
	DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
	DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
#endif
```

Crucially, the enum *also* shrinks. So, in theory, the array size and the enum values are still in sync. Hold on... there's no code that *directly* uses the `enum damos_action` as an array index. That would be the only way a change in enum size would break things. I checked for it. Nothing. 

I then considered tracing, thinking, "Could `trace_damon_aggregated()` be exposing `enum damos_action` directly, thus creating an unstable ABI?" But thankfully, there's no tracepoint exposing `enum damos_action`.

Aha! I neglected the ABI docs for the DAMON sysfs interface. And there's the answer. The sysfs exposes `damos_action` using strings via `damos_sysfs_action_names[]`. It maps the `action` to a string, and `action_show()` *emits* that string. So we are good.

Finally, just to be thorough, I had to check other documentation for usage. `Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst` mentions them, and it seems the diff only updates the design documentation and not the usage. I need to make sure `usage.rst` lists the available actions for consistency.

    "thought": "**Analyzing DAMON Selftest Behavior with and without THP**

Okay, so I've been digging into this DAMON selftest code, and I see a potential issue with how it handles the `action` integer within the schemes. Specifically, in `tools/testing/selftests/damon/drgn_dump_damon_status.py`, it just dumps the `action` as an integer. This seems straightforward, right?

But, the problem arises in `tools/testing/selftests/damon/sysfs.py`. This script *verifies* the dumped `action` against an expected value, comparing `dump['action']` with what's defined in `action_val`. Now, if `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` (THP) is disabled, the mappings in `action_val` are wrong. For example, if a user writes "lru_prio" through sysfs, the kernel maps it to `DAMOS_LRU_PRIO`, which is `3` *if* THP is *disabled*. But, `sysfs.py` expects "lru_prio" to correspond to `5`, based on a hardcoded mapping that *doesn't* account for THP's effect. The drgn dump will return `3`, leading to a failure of the `assert_scheme_committed` tests when the action is `lru_prio`.

I checked if the test writes `hugepage` actions. And it's only run if `drgn` is available. That's a concern. It breaks the selftest if `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled, which is not ideal. This happens because `drgn` reads the memory. The `scheme.action` comes from sysfs, which the test constructs. The default action is 'stat' or something. If the test writes `stat`, that will become `9` in Python but `7` in the kernel *without* THP, which will cause the selftest to fail.

I checked another selftest: `damos_quota.py`. It also has a similar issue, even though it doesn't use `drgn` directly to read the values. The tests uses `sysfs` writes which are translated to kernel integers. The important line is within `sysfs.py` where it calls `dump_damon_status_dict` which uses `drgn_dump_damon_status.py`. So, if `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled, this test will fail as well.

The bigger question is: is this expected? Selftests should ideally handle missing config options, or at least avoid hardcoding enum values that change based on config. By conditionalizing the enum's values, it has made the `enum damos_action` config-dependent, which means any external tool (like `drgn` scripts) that hardcodes those enum values will break.

I'm wondering why they chose to use `#ifdef` *inside* the `enum`. Why not just ignore `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` when building the `damos_sysfs_action_names` array, or return `-EINVAL` in sysfs? If they didn't `#ifdef` the enum, `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` would still be `3` and `4` respectively, even if the strings were not available, or returning an error if there was an attempt to use one of them. The original author's explanation was: "MADV_HUGEPAGE and MADV_NOHUGEPAGE are guarded and they are not available when compiling the kernel without TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE option. Therefore, DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE shouldn't be available to the user either."

However, to make them unavailable to the *user*, they only needed to `#ifdef` them in `damos_sysfs_action_names[]`. `#ifdef`ing them within the enum changes the *internal* integer values of subsequent actions, which causes problems.

Does this break userspace ABI? The sysfs ABI relies on strings (like `"pageout"`, `"lru_prio"`), so the *integer* values of the enum are NOT part of the ABI. But, the `drgn` scripts in the selftests are reading the raw integers and are relying on these *exact* values. That's the core issue, and I need to confirm this by double-checking `sysfs.py`.

    "thought": "**DAMON Action Enum and Potential ABI Instability**

Okay, so I'm looking at these DAMON documentation translations, specifically the usage guides. It seems like a recent patch that updates `design.rst` might have missed updating the usage guides (`zh_TW` and `zh_CN` versions). Generally, documentation is a bit less critical for translations in the grand scheme of things, but something about this nags at me.

The core change in `design.rst` is about the `hugepage` action and transparent hugepages. It now mentions a dependency on `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`. I start wondering if this isn't just a documentation issue. Since `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` is conditionally compiled, what does this actually *mean* for string matching in the DAMON API? What if a user sets an action in a DAMON module like `damon_reclaim`?

I then look into what actions are being exposed by `damon_reclaim` and `damon_lru_sort`. Okay, `damon_reclaim` is limited to `PAGEOUT` and `COLD`, which seems safe. `damon_lru_sort` uses `LRU_PRIO` and `LRU_DEPRIO`.

Then I dig into `include/linux/damon.h`. Ah, here's the enum `damos_action`. Bingo! Now I see it. If `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is *not* set, the values of the enum change! This could be a huge problem.

Let me think this through. With `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` enabled (which is the default), the enum looks like this: `DAMOS_WILLNEED = 0`, `DAMOS_COLD = 1`, `DAMOS_PAGEOUT = 2`, `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE = 3`, `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE = 4`, then `DAMOS_LRU_PRIO = 5`, and so on.

However, *without* `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`, the enum values shift up: `DAMOS_WILLNEED = 0`, `DAMOS_COLD = 1`, `DAMOS_PAGEOUT = 2`, but then `DAMOS_LRU_PRIO` is suddenly `3`, and `DAMOS_LRU_DEPRIO` is `4`. This is a *major* ABI concern.

But wait. DAMON configuration via sysfs uses strings like `"hugepage"` and `"lru_prio"`. That sounds safe at first. But what about internal usages, like this `damon_sysfs_scheme_alloc(DAMOS_STAT, 0)` call? This is where it gets hairy. The `DAMOS_STAT` value is hardcoded, so the integer value changes. Although it's compiled into the kernel so internal values match, are there any user-space-exposed values like integers?

Okay, I test with `/sys/kernel/mm/damon/admin/kdamonds/0/contexts/0/schemes/0/action`. The string is what is exposed. Phew!

Now, I think I need to look at `trace_damos_before_apply`. I wonder if there is an integer value exposed there.

The user interacts with sysfs via strings, such as writing "hugepage" to the action file.     "thought": "**Analyzing DAMON's Hugepage Handling**

Okay, so I'm looking at DAMON's hugepage support. The code declares the enums related to hugepages, but no array sizes are directly using these enum values. That's fine, as expected. I need to figure out why I'm getting an error in my setup.

First, I noticed that `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` isn't included in the logging within `trace_damos_before_apply` or any other logging statements, which is a bit strange. In fact, `trace_damos_before_apply` doesn't print the `action` field at all. I need to consider why this is.

Then, I'm reminded of this commit message: it specifies that `MADV_HUGEPAGE` and `MADV_NOHUGEPAGE` are conditional on the `TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` kernel config option. Therefore, `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` should also be unavailable to the user if the kernel is compiled without `TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`. That's an important constraint.

However, a quick check of `mm/damon/vaddr.c` reveals that `damon_va_apply_scheme` still includes:

```c
#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
	case DAMOS_HUGEPAGE:
		madv_action = MADV_HUGEPAGE;
		break;
	case DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE:
		madv_action = MADV_NOHUGEPAGE;
		break;
#endif
```

I'll check `mm/damon/paddr.c` too. Yep, `DAMOS_PAGEOUT` is there, but `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` is not. This makes sense: `MADV_HUGEPAGE` is only relevant for virtual address (vaddr) and fixed virtual address (fvaddr) operations sets.

Now, a crucial question: does the fixed virtual address operations set actually use `damon_va_apply_scheme`? Yes, it does. That's good to know.

Next, I need to consider `damos_sysfs_action_names[]`. It contains this:

```c
static struct damos_sysfs_action_name damos_sysfs_action_names[] = {
	{
		.action = DAMOS_WILLNEED,
		.name = "willneed",
	},
	{
		.action = DAMOS_COLD,
		.name = "cold",
	},
	{
		.action = DAMOS_PAGEOUT,
		.name = "pageout",
	},
#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE
	{
		.action = DAMOS_HUGEPAGE,
		.name = "hugepage",
	},
	{
		.action = DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,
		.name = "nohugepage",
	},
#endif
...
```

The issue comes up if `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled. What if someone tries to read from sysfs where `scheme->action` is somehow set to `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE`? How could that even happen?
A scheme is allocated with `action = DAMOS_STAT` by default. And the user writes a string to set the action. If THP is off, writing `"hugepage"` will return `-EINVAL`. The action can only be set to one of the valid enum values. That explains it.

Finally, I'll quickly check `include/linux/damon.h` one more time, just to be sure. Nothing significant there that I've missed.

    "content": "{"content":" * @DAMOS_COLD:\t\tCall ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_COLD.\n * @DAMOS_PAGEOUT:\tReclaim the region.\n * @DAMOS_HUGEPAGE:\tCall ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_HUGEPAGE.\n * @DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE:\tCall ``madvise()`` for the region with MADV_NOHUGEPAGE.\n * @DAMOS_LRU_PRIO:\tPrioritize the region on its LRU lists.\n * @DAMOS_LRU_DEPRIO:\tDeprioritize the region on its LRU lists.\n * @DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT:  Migrate the regions prioritizing warmer regions.\n * @DAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD:\tMigrate the regions prioritizing colder regions.\n * @DAMOS_STAT:\t\tDo nothing but count the stat.\n * @NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS:\tTotal number of DAMOS actions\n *\n * The support of each action is up to running &struct damon_operations.\n * Refer to 'Operation Action' section of Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst for\n * status of the supports.\n *\n * Note that DAMOS_PAGEOUT doesn't trigger demotions.\n */\nenum damos_action {\n\tDAMOS_WILLNEED,\n\tDAMOS_COLD,\n\tDAMOS_PAGEOUT,\n#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE\n\tDAMOS_HUGEPAGE,\n\tDAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE,\n#endif\n\tDAMOS_LRU_PRIO,\n\tDAMOS_LRU_DEPRIO,\n\tDAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT,\n\tDAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD,\n\tDAMOS_STAT,\t\t/* Do nothing but only record the stat */\n\tNR_DAMOS_ACTIONS,\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damos_quota_goal_metric - Represents the metric to be used as the goal\n *\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_USER_INPUT:\tUser-input value.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_SOME_MEM_PSI_US:\tSystem level some memory PSI in us.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_USED_BP:\tMemUsed ratio of a node.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP:\tMemFree ratio of a node.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP:\tMemUsed ratio of a node for a cgroup.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP:\tMemFree ratio of a node for a cgroup.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_ACTIVE_MEM_BP:\t\tActive to total LRU memory ratio.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_INACTIVE_MEM_BP:\tInactive to total LRU memory ratio.\n * @NR_DAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_METRICS:\tNumber of DAMOS quota goal metrics.\n *\n * Metrics equal to larger than @NR_DAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_METRICS are unsupported.\n */\nenum damos_quota_goal_metric {\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_USER_INPUT,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_SOME_MEM_PSI_US,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_USED_BP,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_USED_BP,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_FREE_BP,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_ACTIVE_MEM_BP,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_INACTIVE_MEM_BP,\n\tNR_DAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_METRICS,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_quota_goal - DAMOS scheme quota auto-tuning goal.\n * @metric:\t\tMetric to be used for representing the goal.\n * @target_value:\tTarget value of @metric to achieve with the tuning.\n * @current_value:\tCurrent value of @metric.\n * @last_psi_total:\tLast measured total PSI\n * @nid:\t\tNode id.\n * @memcg_id:\t\tMemcg id.\n * @list:\t\tList head for siblings.\n *\n * Data structure for getting the current score of the quota tuning goal.  The\n * score is calculated by how close @current_value and @target_value are.  Then\n * the score is entered to DAMON's internal feedback loop mechanism to get the\n * auto-tuned quota.\n *\n * If @metric is DAMOS_QUOTA_USER_INPUT, @current_value should be manually\n * entered by the user, probably inside the kdamond callbacks.  Otherwise,\n * DAMON sets @current_value with self-measured value of @metric.\n *\n * If @metric is DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_{USED,FREE}_BP, @nid represents the node\n * id of the target node to account the used/free memory.\n *\n * If @metric is DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEMCG_{USED,FREE}_BP, @nid and @memcg_id\n * represents the node id and the cgroup to account the used memory for.\n */\nstruct damos_quota_goal {\n\tenum damos_quota_goal_metric metric;\n\tunsigned long target_value;\n\tunsigned long current_value;\n\t/* metric-dependent fields */\n\tunion {\n\t\tu64 last_psi_total;\n\t\tstruct {\n\t\t\tint nid;\n\t\t\tu64 memcg_id;\n\t\t};\n\t};\n\tstruct list_head list;\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damos_quota_goal_tuner - Goal-based quota tuning logic.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_TUNER_CONSIST:\tAim long term consistent quota.\n * @DAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_TUNER_TEMPORAL:\tAim zero quota asap.\n */\nenum damos_quota_goal_tuner {\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_TUNER_CONSIST,\n\tDAMOS_QUOTA_GOAL_TUNER_TEMPORAL,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_quota - Controls the aggressiveness of the given scheme.\n * @reset_interval:\tCharge reset interval in milliseconds.\n * @ms:\t\t\tMaximum milliseconds that the scheme can use.\n * @sz:\t\t\tMaximum bytes of memory that the action can be applied.\n * @goals:\t\tHead of quota tuning goals (&damos_quota_goal) list.\n * @goal_tuner:\t\tGoal-based @esz tuning algorithm to use.\n * @esz:\t\tEffective size quota in bytes.\n *\n * @weight_sz:\t\tWeight of the region's size for prioritization.\n * @weight_nr_accesses:\tWeight of the region's nr_accesses for prioritization.\n * @weight_age:\t\tWeight of the region's age for prioritization.\n *\n * To avoid consuming too much CPU time or IO resources for applying the\n * &struct damos->action to large memory, DAMON allows users to set time and/or\n * size quotas.  The quotas can be set by writing non-zero values to &ms and\n * &sz, respectively.  If the time quota is set, DAMON tries to use only up to\n * &ms milliseconds within &reset_interval for applying the action.  If the\n * size quota is set, DAMON tries to apply the action only up to &sz bytes\n * within &reset_interval.\n *\n * To convince the different types of quotas and goals, DAMON internally\n * converts those into one single size quota called \"effective quota\".  DAMON\n * internally uses it as the only one real quota.  The conversion is made as\n * follows.\n *\n * The time quota is transformed to a size quota using estimated throughput of\n * the scheme's action.  DAMON then compares it against &sz and uses smaller\n * one as the effective quota.\n *\n * If @goals is not empty, DAMON calculates yet another size quota based on the\n * goals using its internal feedback loop algorithm, for every @reset_interval.\n * Then, if the new size quota is smaller than the effective quota, it uses the\n * new size quota as the effective quota.\n *\n * The resulting effective size quota in bytes is set to @esz.\n *\n * For selecting regions within the quota, DAMON prioritizes current scheme's\n * target memory regions using the &struct damon_operations->get_scheme_score.\n * You could customize the prioritization logic by setting &weight_sz,\n * &weight_nr_accesses, and &weight_age, because monitoring operations are\n * encouraged to respect those.\n */\nstruct damos_quota {\n\tunsigned long reset_interval;\n\tunsigned long ms;\n\tunsigned long sz;\n\tstruct list_head goals;\n\tenum damos_quota_goal_tuner goal_tuner;\n\tunsigned long esz;\n\n\tunsigned int weight_sz;\n\tunsigned int weight_nr_accesses;\n\tunsigned int weight_age;\n\n/* private: */\n\t/* For throughput estimation */\n\tunsigned long total_charged_sz;\n\tunsigned long total_charged_ns;\n\n\t/* For charging the quota */\n\tunsigned long charged_sz;\n\tunsigned long charged_from;\n\tstruct damon_target *charge_target_from;\n\tunsigned long charge_addr_from;\n\n\t/* For prioritization */\n\tunsigned int min_score;\n\n\t/* For feedback loop */\n\tunsigned long esz_bp;\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damos_wmark_metric - Represents the watermark metric.\n *\n * @DAMOS_WMARK_NONE:\t\tIgnore the watermarks of the given scheme.\n * @DAMOS_WMARK_FREE_MEM_RATE:\tFree memory rate of the system in [0,1000].\n * @NR_DAMOS_WMARK_METRICS:\tTotal number of DAMOS watermark metrics\n */\nenum damos_wmark_metric {\n\tDAMOS_WMARK_NONE,\n\tDAMOS_WMARK_FREE_MEM_RATE,\n\tNR_DAMOS_WMARK_METRICS,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_watermarks - Controls when a given scheme should be activated.\n * @metric:\tMetric for the watermarks.\n * @interval:\tWatermarks check time interval in microseconds.\n * @high:\tHigh watermark.\n * @mid:\tMiddle watermark.\n * @low:\tLow watermark.\n *\n * If &metric is &DAMOS_WMARK_NONE, the scheme is always active.  Being active\n * means DAMON does monitoring and applying the action of the scheme to\n * appropriate memory regions.  Else, DAMON checks &metric of the system for at\n * least every &interval microseconds and works as below.\n *\n * If &metric is higher than &high, the scheme is inactivated.  If &metric is\n * between &mid and &low, the scheme is activated.  If &metric is lower than\n * &low, the scheme is inactivated.\n */\nstruct damos_watermarks {\n\tenum damos_wmark_metric metric;\n\tunsigned long interval;\n\tunsigned long high;\n\tunsigned long mid;\n\tunsigned long low;\n\n/* private: */\n\tbool activated;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_stat - Statistics on a given scheme.\n * @nr_tried:\tTotal number of regions that the scheme is tried to be applied.\n * @sz_tried:\tTotal size of regions that the scheme is tried to be applied.\n * @nr_applied:\tTotal number of regions that the scheme is applied.\n * @sz_applied:\tTotal size of regions that the scheme is applied.\n * @sz_ops_filter_passed:\n *\t\tTotal bytes that passed ops layer-handled DAMOS filters.\n * @qt_exceeds: Total number of times the quota of the scheme has exceeded.\n * @nr_snapshots:\n *\t\tTotal number of DAMON snapshots that the scheme has tried.\n *\n * \"Tried an action to a region\" in this context means the DAMOS core logic\n * determined the region as eligible to apply the action.  The access pattern\n * (&struct damos_access_pattern), quotas (&struct damos_quota), watermarks\n * (&struct damos_watermarks) and filters (&struct damos_filter) that handled\n * on core logic can affect this.  The core logic asks the operation set\n * (&struct damon_operations) to apply the action to the region.\n *\n * \"Applied an action to a region\" in this context means the operation set\n * (&struct damon_operations) successfully applied the action to the region, at\n * least to a part of the region.  The filters (&struct damos_filter) that\n * handled on operation set layer and type of the action and pages of the\n * region can affect this.  For example, if a filter is set to exclude\n * anonymous pages and the region has only anonymous pages, the region will be\n * failed at applying the action.  If the action is &DAMOS_PAGEOUT and all\n * pages of the region are already paged out, the region will be failed at\n * applying the action.\n */\nstruct damos_stat {\n\tunsigned long nr_tried;\n\tunsigned long sz_tried;\n\tunsigned long nr_applied;\n\tunsigned long sz_applied;\n\tunsigned long sz_ops_filter_passed;\n\tunsigned long qt_exceeds;\n\tunsigned long nr_snapshots;\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damos_filter_type - Type of memory for &struct damos_filter\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ANON:\tAnonymous pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ACTIVE:\tActive pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_MEMCG:\tSpecific memcg's pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_YOUNG:\tRecently accessed pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_HUGEPAGE_SIZE:\tPage is part of a hugepage.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_UNMAPPED:\tUnmapped pages.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ADDR:\tAddress range.\n * @DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_TARGET:\tData Access Monitoring target.\n * @NR_DAMOS_FILTER_TYPES:\tNumber of filter types.\n *\n * The anon pages type and memcg type filters are handled by underlying\n * &struct damon_operations as a part of scheme action trying, and therefore\n * accounted as 'tried'.  In contrast, other types are handled by core layer\n * before trying of the action and therefore not accounted as 'tried'.\n *\n * The support of the filters that handled by &struct damon_operations depend\n * on the running &struct damon_operations.\n * &enum DAMON_OPS_PADDR supports both anon pages type and memcg type filters,\n * while &enum DAMON_OPS_VADDR and &enum DAMON_OPS_FVADDR don't support any of\n * the two types.\n */\nenum damos_filter_type {\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ANON,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ACTIVE,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_MEMCG,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_YOUNG,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_HUGEPAGE_SIZE,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_UNMAPPED,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ADDR,\n\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_TARGET,\n\tNR_DAMOS_FILTER_TYPES,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_filter - DAMOS action target memory filter.\n * @type:\tType of the target memory.\n * @matching:\tWhether this is for @type-matching memory.\n * @allow:\tWhether to include or exclude the @matching memory.\n * @memcg_id:\tMemcg id of the question if @type is DAMOS_FILTER_MEMCG.\n * @addr_range:\tAddress range if @type is DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_ADDR.\n * @target_idx:\tIndex of the &struct damon_target of\n *\t\t&damon_ctx->adaptive_targets if @type is\n *\t\tDAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_TARGET.\n * @sz_range:\tSize range if @type is DAMOS_FILTER_TYPE_HUGEPAGE_SIZE.\n * @list:\tList head for siblings.\n *\n * Before applying the &damos->action to a memory region, DAMOS checks if each\n * byte of the region matches to this given condition and avoid applying the\n * action if so.  Support of each filter type depends on the running &struct\n * damon_operations and the type.  Refer to &enum damos_filter_type for more\n * details.\n */\nstruct damos_filter {\n\tenum damos_filter_type type;\n\tbool matching;\n\tbool allow;\n\tunion {\n\t\tu64 memcg_id;\n\t\tstruct damon_addr_range addr_range;\n\t\tint target_idx;\n\t\tstruct damon_size_range sz_range;\n\t};\n\tstruct list_head list;\n};\n\nstruct damon_ctx;\nstruct damos;\n\n/**\n * struct damos_walk_control - Control damos_walk().\n *\n * @walk_fn:\tFunction to be called back for each region.\n * @data:\tData that will be passed to walk functions.\n *\n * Control damos_walk(), which requests specific kdamond to invoke the given\n * function to each region that eligible to apply actions of the kdamond's\n * schemes.  Refer to damos_walk() for more details.\n */\nstruct damos_walk_control {\n\tvoid (*walk_fn)(void *data, struct damon_ctx *ctx,\n\t\t\tstruct damon_target *t, struct damon_region *r,\n\t\t\tstruct damos *s, unsigned long sz_filter_passed);\n\tvoid *data;\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/* informs if the kdamond finished handling of the walk request */\n\tstruct completion completion;\n\t/* informs if the walk is canceled. */\n\tbool canceled;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_access_pattern - Target access pattern of the given scheme.\n * @min_sz_region:\tMinimum size of target regions.\n * @max_sz_region:\tMaximum size of target regions.\n * @min_nr_accesses:\tMinimum ``->nr_accesses`` of target regions.\n * @max_nr_accesses:\tMaximum ``->nr_accesses`` of target regions.\n * @min_age_region:\tMinimum age of target regions.\n * @max_age_region:\tMaximum age of target regions.\n */\nstruct damos_access_pattern {\n\tunsigned long min_sz_region;\n\tunsigned long max_sz_region;\n\tunsigned int min_nr_accesses;\n\tunsigned int max_nr_accesses;\n\tunsigned int min_age_region;\n\tunsigned int max_age_region;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos_migrate_dests - Migration destination nodes and their weights.\n * @node_id_arr:\tArray of migration destination node ids.\n * @weight_arr:\t\tArray of migration weights for @node_id_arr.\n * @nr_dests:\t\tLength of the @node_id_arr and @weight_arr arrays.\n *\n * @node_id_arr is an array of the ids of migration destination nodes.\n * @weight_arr is an array of the weights for those.  The weights in\n * @weight_arr are for nodes in @node_id_arr of same array index.\n */\nstruct damos_migrate_dests {\n\tunsigned int *node_id_arr;\n\tunsigned int *weight_arr;\n\tsize_t nr_dests;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damos - Represents a Data Access Monitoring-based Operation Scheme.\n * @pattern:\t\tAccess pattern of target regions.\n * @action:\t\t&damos_action to be applied to the target regions.\n * @apply_interval_us:\tThe time between applying the @action.\n * @quota:\t\tControl the aggressiveness of this scheme.\n * @wmarks:\t\tWatermarks for automated (in)activation of this scheme.\n * @migrate_dests:\tDestination nodes if @action is \"migrate_{hot,cold}\".\n * @target_nid:\t\tDestination node if @action is \"migrate_{hot,cold}\".\n * @core_filters:\tAdditional set of &struct damos_filter for &action.\n * @ops_filters:\tops layer handling &struct damos_filter objects list.\n * @last_applied:\tLast @action applied ops-managing entity.\n * @stat:\t\tStatistics of this scheme.\n * @max_nr_snapshots:\tUpper limit of nr_snapshots stat.\n * @list:\t\tList head for siblings.\n *\n * For each @apply_interval_us, DAMON finds regions which fit in the\n * &pattern and applies &action to those. To avoid consuming too much\n * CPU time or IO resources for the &action, &quota is used.\n *\n * If @apply_interval_us is zero, &damon_attrs->aggr_interval is used instead.\n *\n * To do the work only when needed, schemes can be activated for specific\n * system situations using &wmarks.  If all schemes that registered to the\n * monitoring context are inactive, DAMON stops monitoring either, and just\n * repeatedly checks the watermarks.\n *\n * @migrate_dests specifies multiple migration target nodes with different\n * weights for migrate_hot or migrate_cold actions.  @target_nid is ignored if\n * this is set.\n *\n * @target_nid is used to set the migration target node for migrate_hot or\n * migrate_cold actions, and @migrate_dests is unset.\n *\n * Before applying the &action to a memory region, &struct damon_operations\n * implementation could check pages of the region and skip &action to respect\n * &core_filters\n *\n * The minimum entity that @action can be applied depends on the underlying\n * &struct damon_operations.  Since it may not be aligned with the core layer\n * abstract, namely &struct damon_region, &struct damon_operations could apply\n * @action to same entity multiple times.  Large folios that underlying on\n * multiple &struct damon region objects could be such examples.  The &struct\n * damon_operations can use @last_applied to avoid that.  DAMOS core logic\n * unsets @last_applied when each regions walking for applying the scheme is\n * finished.\n *\n * After applying the &action to each region, &stat is updated.\n *\n * If &max_nr_snapshots is set as non-zero and &stat.nr_snapshots be same to or\n * greater than it, the scheme is deactivated.\n */\nstruct damos {\n\tstruct damos_access_pattern pattern;\n\tenum damos_action action;\n\tunsigned long apply_interval_us;\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/*\n\t * number of sample intervals that should be passed before applying\n\t * @action\n\t */\n\tunsigned long next_apply_sis;\n\t/* informs if ongoing DAMOS walk for this scheme is finished */\n\tbool walk_completed;\n\t/*\n\t * If the current region in the filtering stage is allowed by core\n\t * layer-handled filters.  If true, operations layer allows it, too.\n\t */\n\tbool core_filters_allowed;\n\t/* whether to reject core/ops filters umatched regions */\n\tbool core_filters_default_reject;\n\tbool ops_filters_default_reject;\n/* public: */\n\tstruct damos_quota quota;\n\tstruct damos_watermarks wmarks;\n\tunion {\n\t\tstruct {\n\t\t\tint target_nid;\n\t\t\tstruct damos_migrate_dests migrate_dests;\n\t\t};\n\t};\n\tstruct list_head core_filters;\n\tstruct list_head ops_filters;\n\tvoid *last_applied;\n\tstruct damos_stat stat;\n\tunsigned long max_nr_snapshots;\n\tstruct list_head list;\n};\n\n/**\n * enum damon_ops_id - Identifier for each monitoring operations implementation\n *\n * @DAMON_OPS_VADDR:\tMonitoring operations for virtual address spaces\n * @DAMON_OPS_FVADDR:\tMonitoring operations for only fixed ranges of virtual\n *\t\t\taddress spaces\n * @DAMON_OPS_PADDR:\tMonitoring operations for the physical address space\n * @NR_DAMON_OPS:\tNumber of monitoring operations implementations\n */\nenum damon_ops_id {\n\tDAMON_OPS_VADDR,\n\tDAMON_OPS_FVADDR,\n\tDAMON_OPS_PADDR,\n\tNR_DAMON_OPS,\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damon_operations - Monitoring operations for given use cases.\n *\n * @id:\t\t\t\tIdentifier of this operations set.\n * @init:\t\t\tInitialize operations-related data structures.\n * @update:\t\t\tUpdate operations-related data structures.\n * @prepare_access_checks:\tPrepare next access check of target regions.\n * @check_accesses:\t\tCheck the accesses to target regions.\n * @get_scheme_score:\t\tGet the score of a region for a scheme.\n * @apply_scheme:\t\tApply a DAMON-based operation scheme.\n * @target_valid:\t\tDetermine if the target is valid.\n * @cleanup_target:\t\tClean up each target before deallocation.\n *\n * DAMON can be extended for various address spaces and usages.  For this,\n * users should register the low level operations for their target address\n * space and usecase via the &damon_ctx.ops.  Then, the monitoring thread\n * (&damon_ctx.kdamond) calls @init and @prepare_access_checks before starting\n * the monitoring, @update after each &damon_attrs.ops_update_interval, and\n * @check_accesses, @target_valid and @prepare_access_checks after each\n * &damon_attrs.sample_interval.\n *\n * Each &struct damon_operations instance having valid @id can be registered\n * via damon_register_ops() and selected by damon_select_ops() later.\n * @init should initialize operations-related data structures.  For example,\n * this could be used to construct proper monitoring target regions and link\n * those to @damon_ctx.adaptive_targets.\n * @update should update the operations-related data structures.  For example,\n * this could be used to update monitoring target regions for current status.\n * @prepare_access_checks should manipulate the monitoring regions to be\n * prepared for the next access check.\n * @check_accesses should check the accesses to each region that made after the\n * last preparation and update the number of observed accesses of each region.\n * It should also return max number of observed accesses that made as a result\n * of its update.  The value will be used for regions adjustment threshold.\n * @get_scheme_score should return the priority score of a region for a scheme\n * as an integer in [0, &DAMOS_MAX_SCORE].\n * @apply_scheme is called from @kdamond when a region for user provided\n * DAMON-based operation scheme is found.  It should apply the scheme's action\n * to the region and return bytes of the region that the action is successfully\n * applied.  It should also report how many bytes of the region has passed\n * filters (&struct damos_filter) that handled by itself.\n * @target_valid should check whether the target is still valid for the\n * monitoring.\n * @cleanup_target is called before the target will be deallocated.\n */\nstruct damon_operations {\n\tenum damon_ops_id id;\n\tvoid (*init)(struct damon_ctx *context);\n\tvoid (*update)(struct damon_ctx *context);\n\tvoid (*prepare_access_checks)(struct damon_ctx *context);\n\tunsigned int (*check_accesses)(struct damon_ctx *context);\n\tint (*get_scheme_score)(struct damon_ctx *context,\n\t\t\tstruct damon_region *r, struct damos *scheme);\n\tunsigned long (*apply_scheme)(struct damon_ctx *context,\n\t\t\tstruct damon_target *t, struct damon_region *r,\n\t\t\tstruct damos *scheme, unsigned long *sz_filter_passed);\n\tbool (*target_valid)(struct damon_target *t);\n\tvoid (*cleanup_target)(struct damon_target *t);\n};\n\n/*\n * struct damon_call_control - Control damon_call().\n *\n * @fn:\t\t\tFunction to be called back.\n * @data:\t\tData that will be passed to @fn.\n * @repeat:\t\tRepeat invocations.\n * @return_code:\tReturn code from @fn invocation.\n * @dealloc_on_cancel:\tIf @repeat is true, de-allocate when canceled.\n *\n * Control damon_call(), which requests specific kdamond to invoke a given\n * function.  Refer to damon_call() for more details.\n */\nstruct damon_call_control {\n\tint (*fn)(void *data);\n\tvoid *data;\n\tbool repeat;\n\tint return_code;\n\tbool dealloc_on_cancel;\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/* informs if the kdamond finished handling of the request */\n\tstruct completion completion;\n\t/* informs if the kdamond canceled @fn infocation */\n\tbool canceled;\n\t/* List head for siblings. */\n\tstruct list_head list;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damon_intervals_goal - Monitoring intervals auto-tuning goal.\n *\n * @access_bp:\t\tAccess events observation ratio to achieve in bp.\n * @aggrs:\t\tNumber of aggregations to achieve @access_bp within.\n * @min_sample_us:\tMinimum resulting sampling interval in microseconds.\n * @max_sample_us:\tMaximum resulting sampling interval in microseconds.\n *\n * DAMON automatically tunes &damon_attrs->sample_interval and\n * &damon_attrs->aggr_interval aiming the ratio in bp (1/10,000) of\n * DAMON-observed access events to theoretical maximum amount within @aggrs\n * aggregations be same to @access_bp.  The logic increases\n * &damon_attrs->aggr_interval and &damon_attrs->sampling_interval in same\n * ratio if the current access events observation ratio is lower than the\n * target for each @aggrs aggregations, and vice versa.\n *\n * If @aggrs is zero, the tuning is disabled and hence this struct is ignored.\n */\nstruct damon_intervals_goal {\n\tunsigned long access_bp;\n\tunsigned long aggrs;\n\tunsigned long min_sample_us;\n\tunsigned long max_sample_us;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damon_attrs - Monitoring attributes for accuracy/overhead control.\n *\n * @sample_interval:\t\tThe time between access samplings.\n * @aggr_interval:\t\tThe time between monitor results aggregations.\n * @ops_update_interval:\tThe time between monitoring operations updates.\n * @intervals_goal:\t\tIntervals auto-tuning goal.\n * @min_nr_regions:\t\tThe minimum number of adaptive monitoring\n *\t\t\t\tregions.\n * @max_nr_regions:\t\tThe maximum number of adaptive monitoring\n *\t\t\t\tregions.\n *\n * For each @sample_interval, DAMON checks whether each region is accessed or\n * not during the last @sample_interval.  If such access is found, DAMON\n * aggregates the information by increasing &damon_region->nr_accesses for\n * @aggr_interval time.  For each @aggr_interval, the count is reset.  DAMON\n * also checks whether the target memory regions need update (e.g., by\n * ``mmap()`` calls from the application, in case of virtual memory monitoring)\n * and applies the changes for each @ops_update_interval.  All time intervals\n * are in micro-seconds.  Please refer to &struct damon_operations and &struct\n * damon_call_control for more detail.\n */\nstruct damon_attrs {\n\tunsigned long sample_interval;\n\tunsigned long aggr_interval;\n\tunsigned long ops_update_interval;\n\tstruct damon_intervals_goal intervals_goal;\n\tunsigned long min_nr_regions;\n\tunsigned long max_nr_regions;\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/*\n\t * @aggr_interval to @sample_interval ratio.\n\t * Core-external components call damon_set_attrs() with &damon_attrs\n\t * that this field is unset.  In the case, damon_set_attrs() sets this\n\t * field of resulting &damon_attrs.  Core-internal components such as\n\t * kdamond_tune_intervals() calls damon_set_attrs() with &damon_attrs\n\t * that this field is set.  In the case, damon_set_attrs() just keep\n\t * it.\n\t */\n\tunsigned long aggr_samples;\n};\n\n/**\n * struct damon_ctx - Represents a context for each monitoring.  This is the\n * main interface that allows users to set the attributes and get the results\n * of the monitoring.\n *\n * @attrs:\t\tMonitoring attributes for accuracy/overhead control.\n *\n * For each monitoring context, one kernel thread for the monitoring, namely\n * kdamond, is created.  The pid of kdamond can be retrieved using\n * damon_kdamond_pid().\n *\n * Once started, kdamond runs until explicitly required to be terminated or\n * every monitoring target is invalid.  The validity of the targets is checked\n * via the &damon_operations.target_valid of @ops.  The termination can also be\n * explicitly requested by calling damon_stop().  To know if a kdamond is\n * running, damon_is_running() can be used.\n *\n * While the kdamond is running, all accesses to &struct damon_ctx from a\n * thread other than the kdamond should be made using safe DAMON APIs,\n * including damon_call() and damos_walk().\n *\n * @ops:\tSet of monitoring operations for given use cases.\n * @addr_unit:\tScale factor for core to ops address conversion.\n * @min_region_sz:\tMinimum region size.\n * @adaptive_targets:\tHead of monitoring targets (&damon_target) list.\n * @schemes:\t\tHead of schemes (&damos) list.\n */\nstruct damon_ctx {\n\tstruct damon_attrs attrs;\n\n/* private: internal use only */\n\t/* number of sample intervals that passed since this context started */\n\tunsigned long passed_sample_intervals;\n\t/*\n\t * number of sample intervals that should be passed before next\n\t * aggregation\n\t */\n\tunsigned long next_aggregation_sis;\n\t/*\n\t * number of sample intervals that should be passed before next ops\n\t * update\n\t */\n\tunsigned long next_ops_update_sis;\n\t/*\n\t * number of sample intervals that should be passed before next\n\t * intervals tuning\n\t */\n\tunsigned long next_intervals_tune_sis;\n\t/* for waiting until the execution of the kdamond_fn is started */\n\tstruct completion kdamond_started;\n\t/* for scheme quotas prioritization */\n\tunsigned long *regions_score_histogram;\n\n\t/* lists of &struct damon_call_control */\n\tstruct list_head call_controls;\n\tstruct mutex call_controls_lock;\n\n\tstruct damos_walk_control *walk_control;\n\tstruct mutex walk_control_lock;\n\n\t/* Working thread of the given DAMON context */\n\tstruct task_struct *kdamond;\n\t/* Protects @kdamond field access */\n\tstruct mutex kdamond_lock;\n\n/* public: \\\n\tlist_for_each_entry(r, &t->regions_list, list)\n\n#define damon_for_each_region_from(r, t) \\\n\tlist_for_each_entry_from(r, &t->regions_list, list)\n\n#define damon_for_each_region_safe(r, next, t) \\\n\tlist_for_each_entry_safe(r, next, &t->regions_list, list)\n\n#define damon_for_each_target(t, ctx) \\\n\tlist_for_each_entry(t, &(ctx)->adaptive_targets, list)\n\n#define Okay, so I'm thinking, if we change the values within the `enum damos_action`, could this break things in the tools or scripts that might rely on the integer values? But wait, the integer values aren't exposed directly to userspace as a stable ABI, which is good. Sysfs uses strings like `"hugepage"` and `"nohugepage"`, and the tracepoints don't log the action integer. So changing the enum value is safe in that respect.

Now, let's look at `mm/damon/vaddr.c`. Hmm, I see the `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` cases with `madv_action = MADV_HUGEPAGE` and `madv_action = MADV_NOHUGEPAGE`. But, hold on a second... is there any array indexing going on with `madv_action` or `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` in this file? No, that's fine.

Okay, another scenario. What if someone configures a kernel without `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`, and a DAMON sysfs scheme comes with `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE`? As far as I can see, writing `"hugepage"` to the sysfs `action` file will fail with `-EINVAL`, which is precisely the desired outcome. Good, that's by design.

Now, I'm checking if `madv_action` is properly initialized in `damon_va_apply_scheme`. I believe this is perfectly safe.

What about other DAMON modules, such as `damon_reclaim` or `damon_lru_sort`? But they don't appear to use HUGEPAGE, so no problem there.

Now, what if someone uses the kernel API `damon_new_scheme` directly and passes `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` when `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled? They can't, because `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` is not defined in the enum when `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled. So they would get a compile error. Excellent. Again, the intended behavior.

Okay, is `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` the only guard needed here? Wait, what if `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is enabled but `MADV_HUGEPAGE` is not defined? I see the commit message is mistaken, saying "MADV_HUGEPAGE and MADV_NOHUGEPAGE are guarded and they are not available when compiling the kernel without TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE option." MADV_HWPOISON:\n#endif\n\t\treturn true;\n\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\n/* Can we invoke process_madvise() on a remote mm for the specified behavior? */\nstatic bool always returns with an appropriate lock held. The function will get start and end parameters that\n * cover the overlap between the current vma and the original range.  Any\n * unmapped regions in the original range will result in this function returning\n * -ENOMEM while still calling the madvise_vma_behavior function on all of the\n * existing vmas in the range.  Must be called with the mmap_lock held for\n * reading or writing.\n */\nstatic\nint madvise_walk_vmas(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = madv_behavior->mm;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range *range = &madv_behavior->range;\n\t/* range is updated to span each VMA, so store end of entire range. */\n\tunsigned long last_end = range->end;\n\tint unmapped_error = 0;\n\tint error;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *prev, *vma;\n\n\t/*\n\t * If VMA read lock is supported, apply madvise to a single VMA\n\t * tentatively, avoiding walking VMAs.\n\t */\n\tif (madv_behavior->lock_mode == MADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK &&\n\t    try_vma_read_lock(madv_behavior)) {\n\t\terror = madvise_vma_behavior(madv_behavior);\n\t\tvma_end_read(madv_behavior->vma);\n\t\treturn error;\n\t}\n\n\tvma = find_vma_prev(mm, range->start, &prev);\n\tif (vma && range->start > vma->vm_start)\n\t\tprev = vma;\n\n\tfor (;;) {\n\t\t/* Still start < end. */\n\t\tif (!vma)\n\t\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t\t/* Here start < (last_end|vma->vm_end). */\n\t\tif (range->start < vma->vm_start) {\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * This indicates a gap between VMAs in the input\n\t\t\t * range. This does not cause the operation to abort,\n\t\t\t * rather we simply return -ENOMEM to indicate that this\n\t\t\t * has happened, but carry on.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\tunmapped_error = -ENOMEM;\n\t\t\trange->start = vma->vm_start;\n\t\t\tif (range->start >= last_end)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t/* Here vma->vm_start <= range->start < (last_end|vma->vm_end) */\n\t\trange->end = min(vma->vm_end, last_end);\n\n\t\t/* Here vma->vm_start <= range->start < range->end <= (last_end|vma->vm_end). */\n\t\tmadv_behavior->prev = prev;\n\t\tmadv_behavior->vma = vma;\n\t\terror = madvise_vma_behavior(madv_behavior);\n\t\tif (error)\n\t\t\treturn error;\n\t\tif (madv_behavior->lock_dropped) {\n\t\t\t/* We dropped the mmap lock, we can't ref the VMA. */\n\t\t\tprev = NULL;\n\t\t\tvma = NULL;\n\t\t\tmadv_behavior->lock_dropped = false;\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tvma = madv_behavior->vma;\n\t\t\tprev = vma;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif (vma && range->end < vma->vm_end)\n\t\t\trange->end = vma->vm_end;\n\t\tif (range->end >= last_end)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\tvma = find_vma(mm, vma ? vma->vm_end : range->end);\n\t\trange->start = range->end;\n\t}\n\n\treturn unmapped_error;\n}\n\n/*\n * Any behaviour which results in changes to the vma->vm_flags needs to\n * take mmap_lock for writing. or nothing.\n * @start:\tStart address of madvise-requested address range.\n * @len_in:\tLength of madvise-requested address range.\n * @behavior:\tRequested madvise behavior.\n * @err:\tPointer to store an error code from the check.\n *\n * If the specified behaviour is invalid or nothing would occur, we skip the\n * operation.  This function returns true in the cases, otherwise false.  In\n * the former case we store an error on @err.\n */\nstatic bool madvise_should_skip(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,\n\t\tint behavior, int *err)\n{\n\tif (!is_valid_madvise(start, len_in, behavior)) {\n\t\t*err = -EINVAL;\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\tif (start + PAGE_ALIGN(len_in) == start) {\n\t\t*err = 0;\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\treturn false;\n}\n\nstatic bool is_madvise_populate(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tswitch (madv_behavior->behavior) {\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_READ:\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_WRITE:\n\t\treturn true;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\n/*\n * untagged_addr_remote() assumes mmap_lock is already held. On\n * architectures like x86 and RISC-V, tagging is tricky because each\n * mm may have a different tagging mask. However, we might only hold\n * the per-VMA lock (currently only local processes are supported),\n * so untagged_addr is used to avoid the mmap_lock assertion for\n * local processes.\n */\nstatic inline unsigned long get_untagged_addr(struct mm_struct *mm,\n\t\tunsigned long start)\n{\n\treturn current->mm == mm ? untagged_addr(start) :\n\t\t\t\t   untagged_addr_remote(mm, start);\n}\n\nstatic int madvise_do_behavior(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,\n\t\tstruct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct blk_plug plug;\n\tint error;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range *range = &madv_behavior->range;\n\n\tif (is_memory_failure(madv_behavior)) {\n\t\trange->start = start;\n\t\trange->end = start + len_in;\n\t\treturn madvise_inject_error(madv_behavior);\n\t}\n\n\trange->start = get_untagged_addr(madv_behavior->mm, start);\n\trange->end = range->start + PAGE_ALIGN(len_in);\n\n\tblk_start_plug(&plug);\n\tif (is_madvise_populate(madv_behavior))\n\t\terror = madvise_populate(madv_behavior);\n\telse\n\t\terror = madvise_walk_vmas(madv_behavior);\n\tblk_finish_plug(&plug);\n\treturn error;\n}\n\n/*\n * The madvise(2) system call.\n *\n * Applications can use madvise() to advise the kernel how it should\n * handle paging I/O in this VM area.  The idea is to help the kernel\n * use appropriate read-ahead and caching techniques.  The information\n * provided is advisory only, and can be safely disregarded by the\n * kernel without affecting the correct operation of the application.\n *\n * behavior values:\n *  MADV_NORMAL - the default behavior is to read clusters.  This\n *\t\tresults in some read-ahead and read-behind.\n *  MADV_RANDOM - the system should read the minimum amount of data\n *\t\ton any access, since it is unlikely that the appli-\n *\t\tcation will need more than what it asks for.\n *  MADV_SEQUENTIAL - pages in the given range will probably be accessed\n *\t\tonce, so they can be aggressively read ahead, and\n *\t\tcan be freed soon after they are accessed.\n *  MADV_WILLNEED - the application is notifying the system to read\n *\t\tsome pages ahead.\n *  MADV_DONTNEED - the application is finished with the given range,\n *\t\tso the kernel can free resources associated with it.\n *  MADV_FREE - the application marks pages in the given range as lazy free,\n *\t\twhere actual purges are postponed until memory pressure happens.\n *  MADV_REMOVE - the application wants to free up the given range of\n *\t\tpages and associated backing store.\n *  MADV_DONTFORK - omit this area from child's address space when forking:\n *\t\ttypically, to avoid COWing pages pinned by get_user_pages().\n *  MADV_DOFORK - cancel MADV_DONTFORK: no longer omit this area when forking.\n *  MADV_WIPEONFORK - present the child process with zero-filled memory in this\n *              range after a fork.\n *  MADV_KEEPONFORK - undo the effect of MADV_WIPEONFORK\n *  MADV_HWPOISON - trigger memory error handler as if the given memory range\n *\t\twere corrupted by unrecoverable hardware memory failure.\n *  MADV_SOFT_OFFLINE - try to soft-offline the given range of memory.\n *  MADV_MERGEABLE - the application recommends that KSM try to merge pages in\n *\t\tthis area with pages of identical content from other such areas.\n *  MADV_UNMERGEABLE- cancel MADV_MERGEABLE: no longer merge pages with others.\n *  MADV_HUGEPAGE - the application wants to back the given range by transparent\n *\t\thuge pages in the future. Existing pages might be coalesced and\n *\t\tnew pages might be allocated as THP.\n *  MADV_NOHUGEPAGE - mark the given range as not worth being backed by\n *\t\ttransparent huge pages so the existing pages will not be\n *\t\tcoalesced into THP and new pages will not be allocated as THP.\n *  MADV_COLLAPSE - synchronously coalesce pages into new THP.\n *  MADV_DONTDUMP - the application wants to prevent pages in the given range\n *\t\tfrom being included in its core dump.\n *  MADV_DODUMP - cancel MADV_DONTDUMP: no longer exclude from core dump.\n *  MADV_COLD - the application is not expected to use this memory soon,\n *\t\tdeactivate pages in this range so that they can be reclaimed\n *\t\teasily if memory pressure happens.\n *  MADV_PAGEOUT - the application is not expected to use this memory soon,\n *\t\tpage out the pages in this range immediately.\n *  MADV_POPULATE_READ - populate (prefault) page tables readable by\n *\t\ttriggering read faults if required\n *  MADV_POPULATE_WRITE - populate (prefault) page tables writable by\n *\t\ttriggering write faults if required\n *\n * return values:\n *  zero    - success\n *  -EINVAL - start + len < 0, start is not page-aligned,\n *\t\t\"behavior\" is not a valid value, or application\n *\t\tis attempting to release locked or shared pages,\n *\t\tor the specified address range includes file, Huge TLB,\n *\t\tMAP_SHARED or VMPFNMAP range.\n *  -ENOMEM - Okay, so I'm looking at this patch and how it handles the interaction between DAMON (Data Access MONitor) and Transparent Hugepages (THP). My initial thought process is to understand the potential fallout from disabling THP.

Specifically, I’m focused on `madvise_behavior_valid` in `mm/madvise.c`. It's clear that if `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled, `madvise_behavior_valid` returns `false` for `MADV_HUGEPAGE`. This means that `do_madvise` will return `-EINVAL` if someone tries to use `MADV_HUGEPAGE`.

The patch's logic is straightforward: when THP is disabled, the `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` action is removed from DAMON entirely. This prevents DAMON from calling `madvise` with `MADV_HUGEPAGE` and getting an error, which, frankly, is good. A user gets a clear `-EINVAL` if attempting an unsupported action, rather than an internal error.

Now, let's make sure we haven't missed any API callbacks or potential interface issues. We have to consider how `enum damos_action` is exposed, and what happens to the enum length and the integer values of the later members of the enum when `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` is disabled.

Looking at the code, `damos_sysfs_action_names` in `mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c` is the primary mapping between `enum damos_action` and the sysfs names. I'm also checking other kernel API users of this enum: `mm/damon/reclaim.c`, `mm/damon/lru_sort.c`, etc. But they only use the actions that aren't affected by this change.

I'm verifying the commit message, which states the rationale: "MADV_HUGEPAGE and MADV_NOHUGEPAGE are guarded and they are not available when compiling the kernel without TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE option. Therefore, DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE shouldn't be available to the user either." So, the removal makes sense. If a user tries to enable hugepages in a script on a non-THP kernel, they would get an `-EINVAL` from DAMON, which is the correct and most helpful response.

I'm revisiting how `damos_madvise` would handle the `MADV_HUGEPAGE` case previously: `do_madvise` would return `-EINVAL` when THP was disabled, and now the system rejects the sysfs request. The new approach is a significant UX improvement, as it now rejects configuration when it's not possible, rather than running a bad call.

Next, I'm checking for any arrays sized by the number of actions, but nothing is found. I need to make sure `enum damos_action` isn't passed to or from userspace via any other ABI. The sysfs interface seems to be the primary userspace interaction. There are no ioctls, no netlink, and the tracepoints don't trace the action enum.

Aha! There's `DAMOS_STAT` action in `include/linux/damon.h`. Since `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` is being removed, the integer values of subsequent enum members will shift. However, that's not an issue because kernel internal enums aren't ABI unless exposed as integers (e.g. in `uapi`). Sysfs exposes string names. Tracepoints don't log the action. And, there is no UAPI header for DAMON. `include/linux/damon.h` is not in `include/uapi/`.

Finally, it's essential to ensure the sysfs documentation is updated. I see the patch updates `Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst`. I'm also checking `Documentation/admin-guide/mm/damon/usage.rst`.

Initially, I identified that the test script `tools/testing/selftests/damon/drgn_dump_damon_status.py` (or something similar, the path is a little vague) uses `drgn`, the kernel debugger, to examine internal kernel state. My concern arose that, when `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled, hardcoded enum values used in the test might lead to failures because the underlying enum values for the action flags might change. These are internal definitions, and the tests should not be relying on them.

I honed in on the `action_val` dictionary within `tools/testing/selftests/damon/sysfs.py`, which is an abstraction used by the test. This dictionary maps string representations of DAMON actions to integer values that are actually the underlying enum values in the kernel. My thought process quickly jumped to that the `drgn` script is probably being used to dump the kernel internal structures and compare them to the hardcoded values. I then verified that `sysfs.py` does indeed hardcode these enum integer values.

I realized that if `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled, the enum values like `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` (and, consequently, subsequent enum members) will shift, and these changes are going to make the selftest that validates the action values fail. So, I will report a concern for a test breakage: the selftest hardcodes the integer values of `enum damos_action`, and since `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` are conditionally compiled, the integer values of subsequent enum members will change when `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled. I will also clarify that this will cause the test to fail when comparing the action value dumped via `drgn` against the hardcoded `action_val` dictionary.

I did a double-check for related issues, and confirmed that the dictionary I highlighted contains a typo in the key `'nohugeapge': 4`. I've confirmed that these are indeed what's used in the test with `git show`.

I also briefly looked at another array, `damon_ops_id`, which isn't affected.

I considered what happens if a user disables THP and attempts to set the `action` via sysfs, this will still work because sysfs uses string comparison. This issue *only* happens in the `drgn` validation part of the selftest.

I then considered and discounted any issues with the code itself. The commit correctly implements the intended behavior by hiding `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` when THP is disabled.

Then, I reviewed the code diffs for `mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c` and `mm/damon/vaddr.c`, which correctly use preprocessor directives (`#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`) to conditionally include the hugepage-related actions. All good.
